title: Mass at the Drive-In Theater
gist: Reviewing Stephen Koeth's 'Crabgrass Catholicism,' Aaron Renn argues that postwar suburbanization—not just Vatican II—transformed American Catholicism: explosive Long Island growth forced fewer/larger parishes, broke ethnic-parish ties, collapsed the parish-school model, privatized faith, shifted authority from clergy to laity, and ultimately 'Protestantized' Catholics, reshaping their family structure, class stratification, and political alignment toward emerging conservatism.

insights.claim: The standard account credits Vatican II for twentieth-century Catholic transformation, but postwar suburbanization had profound effects on American Catholic life well before the Council concluded in 1965, making demography a co-equal causal force.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Reframes institutional change as driven by physical/demographic restructuring rather than ideology—a lens applicable to any institution reshaped by where its people live.
insights.quote: the Second Vatican Council ... is presented as the watershed moment. But other forces were at play as well.

insights.claim: Long Island's postwar growth dwarfs any contemporary suburb: Suffolk County grew 141.5% (1950-60) adding 390,655 people, Nassau 93.3% adding 627,406, whereas today's booming Collin County, Texas has never added 300,000 in a decade.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Quantifies the scale of a one-time growth shock; the magnitude is what made institutional adaptation impossible—useful baseline for thinking about hyper-growth strain.
insights.quote: Suffolk County, New York, grew by 141.5 percent during that same period, adding 390,655 people.
insights.locator: Pent-up demand and rapidly emerging supply

insights.claim: Hyper-growth created a double bind: the Catholic population grew too fast to keep up, then quickly hit maturity and decline as fields filled, parents became empty nesters, and the next generation shrank—both Long Island counties essentially flatlined after their boom.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: The boom-then-flatline curve is a generalizable trap: infrastructure built for peak demand becomes a liability when growth reverses—applies to any institution scaling to a demographic wave.
insights.quote: this was followed in quick succession by the challenges of maturity and even decline
insights.locator: This growth profile created an impossible challenge

themes.theme: From ethnic parish to efficient network

insights.claim: Suburbanization amalgamated ethnically distinct Catholicisms (Polish, Italian, Irish) into one body organized by socioeconomic rather than ethnic lines, ending the ethnic parish and shifting from extended-family city networks to nuclear-family suburban households.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Shows how spatial reorganization dissolves identity-based community structures—relevant to how geography reshapes social cohesion.
insights.quote: Postwar suburbanization helped complete the amalgamation of various ethnically inflected forms of Catholicism into one religiously identified body of American Catholicism
insights.locator: In the suburbs, that old order could no longer hold.

insights.claim: Suburbia's low density paradoxically enabled efficiency: car access and the end of ethnic-specific churches let the Church serve growth with fewer, larger 'points of presence'—suburban parishes averaged 3,000 families versus 500 in the city.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Counterintuitive efficiency gain from consolidation; the 6x parish-size jump shows how distribution models change with mobility—a network-design analogy.
insights.quote: the Catholic Church needed fewer “points of presence” (to borrow a term from computer networks) in the suburbs
insights.locator: The difficulties of scaling with growth

themes.theme: Collapse of the parish-school model and privatized faith

insights.claim: Growth outran the ability to build and staff Catholic schools, striking at the core of the American Catholic model (the parish school as the bulwark against Protestant assimilation); the CCD weekly-class model came to predominate, and of 21 parishes founded after the 1957 Rockville Centre diocese, only one ever opened a parochial school.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: A concrete metric of model abandonment under capacity constraints—how an institution quietly replaces its core mechanism when it can't scale it.
insights.quote: Of the twenty-one parishes founded after the diocese’s erection in 1957, only one…ever opened a parochial school.
insights.locator: Eventually, this model came to predominate

insights.claim: Facility shortages forced innovations like Mass in drive-in theaters and homes, which unintentionally detached worshippers from the parish as the source of religious life and pushed faith toward private, family-based devotion over the old public ethnic processions.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Improvised workarounds had durable cultural consequences—shows how stopgap delivery changes can permanently reshape the underlying practice.
insights.quote: they had the unintended effect of detaching people from the parish as the source of religious life, in favor of a more privatized faith
insights.locator: A shortage of facilities resulted in religious innovations.

insights.claim: The staggering size and diverse needs of suburban congregations forced priests to involve laity in building parishes, producing a gradual transfer of authority from clergy to laity that Vatican II later validated via parish councils and school boards.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Demonstrates how scale pressure, not doctrine, can redistribute institutional power—doctrine ratifies what logistics already forced.
insights.quote: Postwar suburbanization accelerated and diversified lay participation ... and led to a gradual transfer of authority from clergy to laity.

insights.claim: Assimilation amounted to the cultural Protestantization of American Catholics—adopting the absolute nuclear family, socioeconomic stratification, and lay involvement, all Anglo-Protestant hallmarks—so that, per Andrew Greeley, there was 'little to distinguish the Catholic suburbanite from his Protestant neighbor.'
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Argues a minority group's distinctiveness erodes structurally through shared living patterns, not conversion—an assimilation mechanism.
insights.quote: little to distinguish the Catholic suburbanite from his Protestant neighbor
insights.locator: that assimilation also represented the cultural Protestantization

themes.theme: Political realignment and the limits of leadership

insights.claim: Assimilation realigned Catholic politics: where Catholics once opposed school prayer as too Protestant, many Long Island Catholics began favoring nonsectarian prayer while Jews remained opposed, presaging Catholic migration toward post-Goldwater conservative Republicanism as Jews stayed Democratic.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Traces a religious-cultural shift into a durable partisan realignment—useful frame for how demographic/cultural change feeds electoral coalitions.
insights.quote: some Catholics began migrating toward emerging conservative Republicanism, particularly in the post-Goldwater era

insights.claim: The author concludes that no slower pace or better Church leadership could have avoided the transformation, because suburbia inherently inclines toward nuclear families, socioeconomic stratification, public-education dominance, and car-reached central facilities.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Argues built environment is destiny for institutions—physical form constrains organizational and cultural outcomes regardless of leadership quality.
insights.quote: The very nature of suburbia inclines toward nuclear families, socioeconomic stratification, the dominance of public education, and central facilities reached by car.
insights.locator: Could a slower pace of suburbanization

insights.claim: Restrictive zoning in the New York region prevented newer exurbs from cannibalizing older Long Island suburbs (unlike declining inner-ring suburbs elsewhere), but at the cost of rendering area housing markets very expensive.
insights.speaker: Aaron M. Renn
insights.so_what: Concrete zoning-to-prices link: supply restriction preserves older neighborhoods while inflating housing costs—relevant to housing-policy and real-estate analysis.
insights.quote: very restrictive zoning practices in the New York region have prevented newer exurbs from cannibalizing older suburbia, at the expense of rendering area housing markets very expensive
insights.locator: Later suburbs, on the other hand, had advantages.
